chunk_summary: This section outlines methods for estimating age-specific energy and protein requirements using Indian reference values, emphasizing adjustments for clinical contexts and dietary gaps in children and infants.
learning_objectives: Understand how to estimate energy and protein needs using Indian reference values (ICMR-NIN).
Learn to adjust dietary prescriptions for clinical contexts such as malnutrition, obesity, or chronic illnesses.
Identify and document dietary gaps using intake comparisons and validated food composition sources.
Apply weight-based approaches for rapid bedside estimations in infants and toddlers.
key_facts: Indian reference values (ICMR-NIN) provide baseline energy and protein needs based on age, sex, and activity level.
Infants require higher energy (kcal/kg) and protein (g/kg/day) compared to toddlers, with needs decreasing as age increases.
Protein targets are higher during recovery from illness or severe malnutrition rehabilitation.
In obesity, calculations should reference ideal body weight rather than actual weight to avoid overprescription.
Dietary gaps can be quantified as a percentage of estimated needs (e.g., meeting ~70% of energy needs).
Locally validated food composition sources (IFCT) should be used for nutrient conversion from household measures.
reference_quote: Estimate daily energy and protein needs using age, sex, weight (or corrected age in preterm infants), and clinical context. Start with Indian reference values (ICMR-NIN) for age and activity, then adjust upward for catch-up growth or high metabolic demand.
document_key_topics: Use of Indian Food Composition Tables (IFCT)
